#ManifestCover
From engineered gym run-ins to a romance that unfolded at its own pace, Allu Sirish (@allusirish) and Nayanika Reddy’s (@nayanika_reddy) story feels less like a whirlwind and more like a rhythm built over time.
“It wasn’t like we suddenly started going on back-to-back dates. It was more of a slow-burn romance,” says Sirish, recalling how a series of chance meetings — and one very intentional gym switch — finally brought them together.
For Nayanika, the turning point was simpler.
“I realised how easy it felt to be around him. Our conversations were natural, and there was always a sense of comfort. Over time, it became clear that we were building something meaningful,” she shares.
Just days before their wedding, the couple comes together for their first editorial shoot, speaking to Manifest about love that grew organically, families that were always one step ahead, and why the most meaningful relationships don’t need grand gestures to feel certain.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
#ManifestDigitalCover | The Shefali Shah Effect
For over three decades, Shefali Shah (@shefalishahofficial) has built a career on instinct, honesty, and an unwavering refusal to fit into boxes. From the unforgettable Pyari in Satya to the formidable DCP Vartika Chaturvedi in Delhi Crime, her performances have never relied on noise — only truth.
“A few years ago, if someone told me a woman in her mid-forties would lead a show, I would have laughed. And if they said it would be me, I would have laughed louder,” she says.
In our latest digital cover, the actor reflects on evolving with time, finding freedom in flawed characters, 25 years of marriage, and why success today means something far quieter than fame.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Mini dresses for the after-party bride.
As much as we love the drama of Indian silhouettes, there’s no denying the effort it takes to move around in a 20 kg lehenga. You may commit to the grandeur for the ceremony or reception, but the after-party is a different story.
Think of it as your cue to switch gears. Something lighter, easier, and far more dance-friendly.
A mini dress lets you dial it down without losing the glamour. Less weight, more movement, and just the right amount of sparkle to keep the night going.

Daughter of couturier JJ Valaya, Hoorvi Valaya (@hoorvi.j.valaya) redefined what personalised Kaleeras can look like at her wedding. Each charm, each detail, each tiny element became a marker of memory, turning a traditional accessory into something far more intimate.
Crafted by Mrinalini Chandra (@mrinalinichandra) , the kaleeras moved beyond ornamentation. They became a quiet archive of her love story—moments, meanings, and memories, all suspended in gold.
Hoorvi’s approach felt less like reinvention and more like evolution. Not bigger. Not louder. Just… more personal.

posts.caption:
The green and cream cap is everywhere. You can buy one at the Masters golf tournament and at 7-Eleven. The athleisure brand Vuori sells one for $48. So did Andy Roddick’s tennis podcast, “Served.” They were $40 but are now sold out.
"In the past couple of years, the two-tone baseball cap has become the red Toyota Camry of the hat market," our fashion reporter Jacob Gallagher wrote. "I saw one. Then I couldn’t stop seeing them."
Look at golf brands (Fore All), basketball lifestyle labels (Monday Hoops Club) and a surf company in landlocked Austin, Texas (Mañana). Team U.S.A. sells one, as did Ray’s Bar in Miami. The Barstool Sports website sells a $38 version that reads “Boy Mom.” Zendaya was photographed in one from Bero, Tom Holland’s nonalcoholic beer brand.
Why has this particular combination become so ubiquitous? At the link in our bio, @jacobwgallagher dives into the trend. Photos by Raymond Hall/GC Images, via Getty Images, Rich Barnes/Getty Images, Raimonda Kulikauskiene/Getty Images, @nytmills

posts.caption:
Tensions between Alex Cooper, the creator and host of the Golden Globe-nominated podcast “Call Her Daddy,” and Alix Earle, the founder of the Reale Actives skin care brand, have seemingly spilled into public view after a year of on-and-off rumors about a falling out.
The Lix vs. Lex feud — between two of the internet’s biggest personalities, who have millions of followers each and homophone names — started last week when Earle reposted a TikTok video that criticized Cooper’s ethics and approach to her business and podcast. On Monday, Cooper posted a video on TikTok, urging Earle to “say what you got to say about me.” Earle responded in the comments: “Okay on it!!”
The drama has been followed obsessively on social media, with each twist and turn being viewed, liked and shared millions of times. Who is involved and what's going on? Read what we know about the drama at the link in our bio. Photos by Neilson Barnard/Getty Images; Daniel Cole/Reuters

posts.caption:
Over the weekend, scores of people headed to Southern California for this year’s Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival. Though sets by performers including Sabrina Carpenter, Sombr and Justin Bieber were a big draw, the lineup has become just one defining feature of the event. For many who attend the festival, seeing musical acts is just as important as being seen at a sponsored party or in a flashy outfit.
For both performers and attendees, the spectacle of Coachella has sartorially translated into ensembles that rarely seem rooted in reality. But that is precisely what makes the festival’s fashion so fun to see. Think of the people on this list not as the best dressed, but as among those whose clothes, whether loudly or quietly, made a memorable impression.
What other looks made our list? Tap the link in our bio to see all 11. Photos by Kevin Mazur/Getty Images, Daniel Cole/Reuters, Amy Harris/Invision, via @apnews, Frazer Harrison/Getty Images, Arturo Holmes/Getty Images

posts.caption:
Begging, manifesting, sheer dumb luck: Influencers will try anything to get someone to pay their way to the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival.
In the social media age, the two-weekend event — the first began on Friday — has become a hot spot for content creators, some of whom attend without ever seeing a single musical act. Brands like the fast-fashion company Revolve and Kendall Jenner’s tequila brand, 818, throw exclusive, invitation-only events at which creators clamor to see and, more crucially, be seen. A creator might drag out posting videos — from buying outfits and packing all the way to the flight home — for as much as a month to capitalize on the attention.
But the all-expenses-paid brand trips to the festival have become more scarce over the years. “Brands have picked up on how thirsty creators are to go to Coachella,” one influencer manager said. “They’re realizing they don’t have to pay them.”
We spoke to content creators about the different tactics, successful and not, they have used to tried to receive an invitation to Coachella. Tap the link in our bio to read what they said. Photo by @ye.fan

posts.caption:
When the costume designer Natasha Newman-Thomas joined HBO’s “Euphoria” for its long-gestating third season, she had a gigantic set of Chuck Taylors to fill. No show has better captured the chaotic, mash-it-all-together style of Gen Z.
Beginning with the first season in 2019, the characters’ trend-overload mélange — translucent tops and navel-revealing shirts, tie-dye and argyle, angel wings and Dickies work pants — became its own blogged-about trend.
Because of a yearslong narrative leap between the second and third seasons of the show, the characters’ past wardrobes have mostly been left behind, except for that of Rue, who is played by Zendaya and around whom “Euphoria” orbits. In a recent interview, Newman-Thomas elaborated on the influences behind each character’s clothes for the new season. Tap the link in our bio to read the full story. Photo by HBO Max
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on April 13, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'Shaping the New Look of Euphoria IIIII'.

posts.caption:
Mandy Lieveld, a former model from the Netherlands, has been working as a walking coach for over 10 years. Her job is to teach models — and non-models — how to confidently traverse a runway. After learning about her teachings, the writer Liana Satenstein was intrigued. “Could I benefit from the Lieveld treatment?” she wondered.
So she booked a lesson. Armed with a pair of Jimmy Choo heels at a studio in Midtown Manhattan, @liana_ava began her 90 minutes of intense instruction with the posture whisperer.
The Lieveld method focuses just as much on the mental aspects of a runway-worthy walk as the physical, frequently using the word “relax” to instruct her students. She offers tricks for everyday scenarios and helpful mnemonics to deal with catwalk variables and help her models feel comfortable. Can her teachings help the average person? At the link in our bio, get an inside look at what a session with @runwaycoach entails. Visuals by @eleanorschmitt
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on April 09, 2026. The writer Liana Satenstein (right) booked a lesson to see if she could learn how to walk like a model.'.

posts.caption:
They say you can tell when a designer loves the women they’re dressing, and that’s pretty obvious with #SarahBurton’s #Givenchy. Her latest collection moved between tailoring and draping, a refelction of the modern day women she is designing for.
One of the most striking looks was the floral dress, an adaptation of a painting by Filipino artist Olan Ventura. Cascading strands of fringe distorted the floral motifs across the dress so they appeared almost blurred or melting.
The headpieces, by @stephenjonesmillinery , were actually Silk T-shirts twisted and wrapped around the models’ heads, forming sculptural shapes reminiscent of Dutch Golden Age portraiture, specifically Rogier van der Weyden’s Woman with a Winged Bonnet.
What did you think of the collection?

posts.caption:
Only a handful of brides, including Gwen Stefani and Melania Trump, have had the privilege of wearing a custom #weddingdress designed by #JohnGalliano - and #KateMoss’s may be one of the most beautiful  ever created.
In 2011, John created a bias-cut silk slip dress adorned with gold embroidery, sheer paneling, and a cascade of sequins inspired by the 1920s and 1930s. At the time, the dress was an interesting choice, as Kate chose John to design it amid his highly publicised fall from grace after being dismissed from #Dior. As a longtime muse and friend, Kate chose to stand by Galliano during a turbulent time in his career.
The dress was also displayed at the Victoria & Albert Museum in London as part of the “Wedding Dresses 1775–2014” exhibition.
Images courtesy of Vogue / Mario Testino / Getty / V&A Museum
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on March 17, 2026. May be an image of wedding and text that says 'Remember When Kate Got Married in Galliano?'.

posts.caption:
#SadieSink’s custom @prada look styled by @mollyddickson got me thinking - is the Regency fashion era back? Emerging in the late 18th and early 19th century, the empire silhouette was inspired by Greco-Roman sculpture and the cultural changes following the French Revolution. Marking a departure from structured court dress, replacing heavy corsetry and panniers.
The Regency revival can partly be influenced by the rise of #Bridgerton, repositioning the empire silhouette as a modern look. Recently, both Emma Stone and #Zendaya wore @louisvuitton looks reminiscent of Regency wear.
This influential era has always been part of fashion, with designers like #JohnGalliano romantically referencing the empire line waist during his tenure at Givenchy and Dior. #JonathanAnderson’s Dior tailoring, waistcoats, high collars, and elongated coats, is also reminiscent of early nineteenth-century dandy dress.
Images courtesy of Getty / Netflix / Givenchy / Prada
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on April 01, 2026. May be an image of gown, dress and text that says 'Is Fashion Returning to the Regency Era?'.

posts.caption:
#KimJones has created some of the most incredible runway show sets during his seven years at #Dior Men, and my all-time favourite has to be Spring 2024. Inspired by #ChristianDior’s lifelong love of gardens, @villaeugenie designed a futuristic “mechanical garden.”
The set combined elements of a laboratory and a spaceship, featuring a glowing, grid-like floor made of hidden panels. Models rose up from beneath the runway on concealed platforms, like flowers growing (complete with floral hats by @stephenjonesmillinery )
The clothes were rich in texture and decoration, including jewels, cannage, and the occasional leopard print. While much of the palette remained classic, there were flashes of neon pink, green, and blue.
images courtesy of Alfredo Piola / Dior / Isidore Montag / Gorunway.com
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by umar on April 16, 2026 tagging @dior, and @kimjonesstudio. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'This Has To Be Dior's Coolest Runway Set Ever'.

posts.caption:
The #SaintLaurent Spring 2026 finale dresses have quickly become some of the most pulled pieces for editorial shoots, seen on #HaileyBieber &  #KylieJenner to name a few. The dramatic, billowing gowns by #AnthonyVaccarello are defined by their oversized ruffles, voluminous skirts, and bold colours.
They seem to be a homage to #YvesSaintLaurent’s couture in the 1970s and 1980s, when he frequently embraced grand, voluminous evening silhouettes adorned with expansive organza ruffles and layered flounces. The exaggerated volume creates instantly dramatic images. The strong colors make them ideal for covers, requiring minimal additional styling. The fact that they are made from nylon means they are perfectly suited for shoots - lighter, more flexible, and easier to transport.
So… who wore it best
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on April 14, 2026. May be an image of magazine, dress and text that says 'This YSL Dress (& PR Team) Is Working Overtime...'.

posts.caption:
For the Met Gala scene in the new #DevilWearsPrada 2, #MirandaPreistley wore a custom #Balenciaga silk taffeta gown by #PierpaoloPiccioli, which was also showcased on the film’s poster.
The dress references #CristóbalBalenciaga’s signature balloon silhouettes, eliminating traditional tailoring and instead relying on fabric manipulation and structure. In this gown, @pppiccioli channels that same idea, allowing the fabric to hold its own form.
Looking ahead to his couture debut, the dress offers clues about what we could expect: craftsmanship and textile innovation, revisiting the structural experimentation that defined Cristóbal’s work and colour, something Piccioli has always embraced. This may also play a more prominent role in Balenciaga’s traditionally restrained palette.
Images courtesy of Macall Polay / 20th Century Studios / Manuel Outumuro / Balenciaga museo / Getty image / Pierpaolo Piccioli / Entertainment Tonight

posts.caption:
#Opinion | Standup comedian Kaneez Surka @kaneezsurka recently opened up about the "beauty of divorce" on a podcast by Aditi Chandanani @therapywith_aditivc. "Oh you MUST get divorced!" Kaneez quipped, getting Aditi all excited about 'life 2.0'. "When you get divorced, society just drops all expectations of you... they give up on you, and the giving up is the best part!" Kaneez expressed.
Divorce used to be a woman's cue to hide way from society; a label that crept into every introduction. She is just a woman anymore; she is a divorcee. But the women of 2026 are rewriting what it means to walk away from an unfulfilling marriage. They are refusing to carry endings like shame, turning divorce into a cultural statement. Earlier, divorce defined the woman. Now, the woman defines the divorce.
A woman who leaves an unhappy marriage disrupts a comforting myth - that self sacrifice is the highest form of femininity. When she says, “This is not working for me,” she exposes how many others are quietly tolerating what does not work for them either. And that can feel threatening to many.  But here is the truth women know in their bones. Divorce doesn't celebrate heartbreak; it is usually the aftermath of it. So why should women carry the shame of something that often requires immense courage? Why should they be the ones apologising for choosing peace?
Read the full article at www.shethepeople.tv | Views expressed by the author are their own.

posts.caption:
Today, if you scroll through social media, among all the chaos of mehendi designs, what you will find is a glimpse of bold red dye – alta, which once got lost in the crowd, is reclaiming its space again. But did it really leave?
As Soundarya S, a yoga practitioner and dancer, says, “Alta has always been there, especially with dancers using it for ages! I don’t really see it as a new trend. It feels more like people are going back to their roots and embracing tradition again.”
Alta, also termed as mahavar or lakshaya rasa, is a vibrant red liquid dye which is said to have originated from the Vedas and Upanishads. It was associated with goddess Lakshmi and holds deep cultural significance of fertility, prosperity, marital status and is a part of solah shringar (16 adornments).
Alta is also a crucial part of classical dances like Odissi and Kathak to highlight the hand gestures and footwork. It remains a deeply rooted part of Eastern Indian states like Bengal, Odisha, and Assam and is used to beautify the hands and feet of women during weddings and festivals.
Read more at www.shethepeople.tv | Article by Ankita Kundu
#alta #mehendi #dance makeup, dance makeup, indian classical dance, alta history, bengali culture, paro devdas
posts.alt_text: Photo by SheThePeopleNEWS on April 15, 2026. May be an image of magazine, poster and text that says 'a shethepeople Paint It Red: Alta Is Making A Symbolic Comeback'.

posts.caption:
#Opinion | The latest flashpoint between Donald Trump and Iran did not just unfold through military warnings or official statements. It spilt directly onto social media, where threats were posted publicly, and responses came back just as fast.
Trump’s Truth Social post set things off as he wrote, “Tuesday will be Power Plant Day, and Bridge Day, all wrapped up in one, in Iran. There will be nothing like it!!! Open the F--kin’ Strait, you crazy bastards, or you’ll be living in Hell - JUST WATCH!”
What followed was not a standard diplomatic pushback. Iranian embassies across countries responded in a way that looked less like statecraft and more like coordinated online trolling. The Iranian embassy in Zimbabwe replied to the demand with a deadpan line “We’ve lost the keys.”
When Trump set a deadline, the response mocked even that: “8 P.M. is not that good. Could you change it to between 1 and 2 P.M., or if possible, 1 and 2 A.M.?” Another exchange leaned into callback humour. When someone asked, “Iran trolling Trump. What year are we in?”, the reply came: “Not in the Stone Age, yet,” referencing Trump’s earlier threat about bombing Iran “back to the Stone Ages.”
Traditional diplomacy relied on carefully worded statements that primarily addressed governments and policy circles. These new responses are built for a completely different audience: the internet. It shows how conflict is no longer contained within formal diplomatic channels. It is unfolding in public real time and in a language built for people to instantly relate to, react to and reshare. For a large part of the global audience, especially younger users, the internet is now the first source of news. They are not waiting for lengthy press briefings or carefully drafted diplomatic notes. They are watching presidents, embassies, and official state handles reply directly to each other on their feeds.
Read full article by @shrutiibedi at www.shethepeople.tv | Views expressed by the author are their own.
#usa #iran USA Iran Israel war Gaza war memes digital war

posts.caption:
A note before you comment: STUDIO gives you access to our free 7 day trial of The Studio of Stylist Elixir, our membership community at £20 a month. We also share free guides and resources on Instagram and a free Substack every Wednesday. 💚
Fashion styling has a rate. It always has. The problem is that this industry has never been transparent about what that rate is, and for decades stylists have absorbed the consequences of that silence.
The work starts before the shoot. The expertise takes years to build. The pressure is carried quietly so nobody else has to feel it. And when that work generates commercial value for a brand, a client, or a campaign, it is not a favour. It is a job. And jobs have rates.
We polled over 1,200 stylists across every sector of the industry because we believe that access to this information should not be a privilege. It should be the starting point.
If you are a stylist who has ever quoted blind, undercharged because you were not sure, or struggled to explain your worth to a client, this is for you.
If you are a client, a brand, or someone who has ever wondered what a fashion stylist actually charges and why, this is for you too.
Comment STUDIO and we will send you access to the full rate breakdown inside The Studio of Stylist Elixir. 💚​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

posts.caption: If something needs such in-depth analysis to be understood than the assignment was a failure! It’s not a dissertation on the politics of 17th century France. It’s CLOTHING! It’s a fashion show and since fashion shows have been able to be seen by millions and consumed in photos and video for decades, those who are not in the loop to see the clothes for real are only left with what we see and to dare to say that just because a design has “References” or inner history negates it from being judged as what it simply LOOKS LIKE, is insultingly arrogant, something that takes 4,000 hours of the most intricate and elaborate technique… CAN STILL LOOK HORRID! All that time does not acquit it of its ugliness or stupidity of design! When all that history and back story sgd references WORK IN CONJUNCTION with the clothes to crest something magical (Blazy/Chanel) then the framework of all that in depth research and application of history is breathtaking, when it’s like JWA/Dior, it just comes across as over focus on ideas that then translate into nothing more than dissertations on a thought than the true purpose of what is happening… MAKING BEAUTIFUL CLOTHES! And for you to suggest that unless you’re erudite and informed of history that you shouldn’t comment is the height of condescension! Anyone can see! And no matter how layered the meaning, if it looks like all that time taken to produce garbage still looks like GARBAGE, it changes not its beauty because you have the knowing of the thought processes and HISTOIRE D’MAISON behind you! It’s pretentious and asinine
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on January 27, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine and poster.

posts.caption:
There is a particular kind of quiet that settles into a fashion styling career when things slow down.
It is not dramatic. Nobody announces it. One week the emails are less frequent. Then a little less. Then you are checking your inbox with a kind of hope that starts to feel embarrassing.
And the whole time you are telling yourself it is temporary. The industry is slow right now. The right people just have not seen the work yet. Something will come.
Most stylists I know have lived inside that quiet at some point. Some of them are living inside it right now.
The hardest thing about it is not the silence itself. It is the story the silence tells you about yourself. That you are not good enough. Not connected enough. Not visible enough.
Here is what I know after years of mentoring stylists.
Visibility in this industry is not something that happens to you. It is something you build. Slowly. Unglamorously. Often without anyone noticing for a while.
And the decision to start building it is the hardest part. Not because it is complicated. Because it requires going first before you feel ready.
This week on the Stylist Elixir Substack I wrote about exactly that. The three things that are actually stopping you from getting booked and the one decision that changes everything.
It is completely free to read.
Comment SEEN below and I will send you the link straight to your DMs. 💚

May be a graphic of poster, magazine and text that says 'stylistelixir.substack.com The Fashion Industry Is Not Going to Come Looking for You Visibility is not something that happens to you. Itis It is something you build. StylistElixir tE Elixir Stylist'.

posts.caption:
The hardest thing about building a fashion styling career is not the work.
It is comparing yourself to someone who started with connections, money, and a head start.
The industry is easier to break into when you already have all of that. That is just true.
But it has never only been for them.
Some of the most interesting careers in this industry have been built by people who worked two jobs to fund the test shoot. Who had no contacts and made them from scratch. Who had no head start and created one.
It is harder. It is not impossible.
This is what we talk about at Stylist Elixir. 💚
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on April 14, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'The hardest thing about building a fashion styling career is not the work. It is comparing yourself to someone who had connections, money, and a head start. Stylist StylistElixir Elixir'.

posts.caption:
The right clients are out there and they are booking stylists right now.
That is not a motivational statement. It is just true. Every single day, clients with briefs and budgets are looking for stylists to work with. The question is not whether those clients exist. The question is whether they can find you when they go looking.
And for so many talented stylists, the answer is no. Not because the work is not good enough. It almost always is. But because being good at the job and being visible to the right people are two completely different things. And this industry does not automatically connect them.
The stylists who get booked consistently are not always the most talented in the room. They are the ones who made it easy to be found. Who made it easy to say yes to. Who positioned themselves so clearly that when the right client came looking, there was no question.
That is not luck. That is something you can actually work on.
If you have been putting yourself out there and still attracting the wrong clients, or no clients at all, the problem is almost never the work. It is the positioning underneath it. And that is a solvable problem.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is our membership platform for fashion stylists. Inside you will find a full section on pitching, positioning and finding the right clients. Tools, frameworks and guides built from real conversations with stylists across every sector of this industry.
First seven days are completely free.
Comment STUDIO and we will send you access.

posts.caption:
ADHD in women often reads as competence: multitasking, perfectionism, emotional labour. Underneath, it’s chronic overwhelm, time blindness, and exhaustion.
Long  framed as a male disorder, ADHD symptoms in women are overlooked, or misread  as stress. The result is years of self-blame before recognition. Masking, hormonal shifts, and the cost of care in India continue to delay diagnosis and limit access to treatment.
The system isn’t missing ADHD in women. It’s been trained not to see it.
Read more at the link in bio
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#ADHD #MentalHealthAwareness #WomensHealth #Neurodiversity #Wellness

posts.caption:
Family in India is ritual, inheritance, and belonging. It is also a negotiation between silence, recognition, and exclusion.
Festivals mark who is called home. For many LGBTQ people, that call does not always come. Disinheritance extends beyond wills. It shapes who is remembered, invited, and named.
The cost is emotional and material. Housing, inheritance, and legitimacy still hinge on heteronormative structures.
Yet kinship persists. Chosen families cook, care, and carry traditions forward. They invest in niblings, sustain intergenerational ties, and reshape belonging on their own terms.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Tejaswi Subramanian (@tejnesss)
#QueerIndia #QueerRelationships #ChosenFamily #LGBTQVoices #TheEstablished

Bodycare products now sit in a grey zone. Compelling, yet still being proven.

posts.caption:
Alia Bhatt (@aliaabhatt) joins Myntra as its ambassador, taking on a role spanning the platform’s largest categories.
Few names operate across fashion and beauty with equal ease. Bhatt’s presence sits comfortably across everyday consumption and global brand ecosystems, making her a natural fit for a platform built on scale and accessibility.
That range matters for a platform like Myntra. It has long led online fashion retail in India, but adjacent categories and higher frequency consumption increasingly shape its growth. It has built its base in fashion, with beauty growing alongside it. Myntra Beauty focuses on making quality-led products more accessible, a shift reflected in its growth.
Myntra doesn’t need a face to introduce it. It has scale. The consumer today moves across high and low, fashion and beauty, all at once. Bhatt fits that behaviour neatly.
She fits seamlessly into how people already shop on Myntra today—fluid, frequent, and across categories. Her alignment sharpens the platform’s core: a consumer journey that moves instinctively between aspiration and access, with no friction in between.

posts.caption:
Ghosting, catfishing, and dating apps fatigue have quietly reshaped modern dating in India.
Singles aren’t swiping in isolation anymore. Friends are stepping in as matchmakers, sounding boards, and red-flag spotters. WhatsApp group chats weigh in, cafés and breweries are hosting mixers again, and even Tinder is experimenting with formats that mirror this shift.
Where parents once played cupid, friends now hold more influence. Emotional compatibility, political alignment, and personality quirks carry more weight than family background or income. Dating has moved from instinct to consultation, from private decisions to something more collective.
Read more at the link in bio
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#Groupchatdating #datingculture #GenZIndia #datingapps #groupchat

posts.caption:
Did Jane Birkin secretly hate the Birkin? The short answer is no. But she definitely didn’t treat it the way the internet does.
She loved the idea of the bag (it was literally designed for her), but she was also very honest about the reality: it was heavy, a little impractical, and not exactly aligned with her effortless, bohemian style. She famously called it “bloody heavy” and joked it probably gave her shoulder problems.
What’s more interesting is how she used it. She covered her Birkins in stickers, beads, and charms, let them wear down, and often overfilled them. You know, treated them like an actual everyday bag, not a museum piece.
And at one point in 2015, she even asked Hermès to remove her name from the crocodile version over animal welfare concerns, although she later continued her association with the brand.
So no, she didn’t hate it. She just didn’t idolize it. Which, ironically, is exactly what made her style so iconic.
#JaneBirkin #Hermes #BirkinBag #FashionHistory #PersonalStyle
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Citizen’s Poste on April 13, 2026. May be an image of one or more people and text that says 'Did Jane Secretly Hate Her Birkin Bag?'.

posts.caption:
I don’t enjoy vintage shopping. There. I said it. For all the romanticization of vintage and archival in fashion over the last few years, I just can’t do it. That said, I also live part-time in Paris—widely considered the unofficial capital of vintage shopping.
So this spring, I took it upon myself to get with the program. I’ve tried the hole-in-the-wall spots, the kilo shops, the friend-of-a-friend’s gatekept recommendations, and the TikTok favorites—but these five are the ones that actually deliver. Whether you’re a vintage enthusiast or not, they’re worth the visit.
Browse the full edit on Substack: The Citizen’s Poste | posteposteposte
#Paris #VintageFashion #ArchivalFashion #ShoppingGuide #Substack

posts.caption:
I’m planning my next trip as we speak because I need to see this—and you do too. The Château du Jonchet, once home to Hubert de Givenchy, is one of those places that feels almost unreal. A 16th-century Renaissance estate, reimagined by Givenchy after he purchased it in 1975 with Philippe Venet, and turned into a masterclass in what he called “monumental simplicity.”
The interiors were insanely curated. Think 18th-century antiques layered with modern pieces, including bronze furniture and sculptures by Diego Giacometti. Outside, it’s full fairytale energy: a rose garden designed by Bunny Mellon, geometric boxwood parterres, a moat, even an artificial lake.
Proof that fashion people don’t just design clothes. They design entire worlds.
#Givenchy #ChateauDuJonchet #FranceTravel #FashionHistory #LuxuryLiving
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Citizen’s Poste on April 11, 2026. May be an image of urn, the Cotswolds and text that says 'I Need to Visit Hubert de Givenchy's Château du Jonchet Right Now'.

Mrs. Nita Ambani honoured the extraordinary artists who brought India’s spirit to life, marking NMACC’s 3rd anniversary. The legendary trio Shankar-Ehsaan-Loy (@shankarehsaanloy) alongside the all-time-favourite Shreya Ghoshal (@shreyaghoshal), Rajasthani folk icon Mame Khan (@mame_khan), Gujarat’s celebrated duo Osman Mir (@osman.mir) & Aamir Mir (@aamir_o_mir) and percussion prodigy Ojas Adhiya (@ojasadhiya), created a soaring tribute to the country’s musical heritage in Ek Sur – Bharat Ki Awaaz at The Grand Theatre(@nmacc.india).
Veteran performer Shekhar Suman (@shekhusuman) celebrated devotional musician Agam Aggarwal(@agam.zen), sitar virtuoso Swayam (@swayam.sitar) and Bharatanatyam luminary Radhe Jaggi (@radhejaggi), who added to the festivities at NMACC’s Studio Theatre and The Cube, were also felicitated as part of the celebrations.
To more experiences that leave us spellbound!

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee (@bajpayee.manoj) is ridiculously youthful-looking for a 50-something. He’s trim, his face glows, he’s light on his feet. His body of work is already impressive and curated, and his physical self suggests the same level of care and attention. “You sacrifice a lot and don’t compromise with your daily routine,” says Bajpayee. It’s been 17 years since he started skipping dinners, fitness advice that has now become a habit. His work follows the same rigour.
From the beginning, there’s been a palpable hunger in Bajpayee’s progression—an unwavering journey towards excellence that has been present ever since he became the centrepiece of Ram Gopal Varma’s classic crime drama ‘Satya’ as the flamboyant gangster Bhiku Mhatre in 1998. Over the years, the actor became the name to go to for films and shows that demanded an assiduous unpacking of characters with his ability to not just inhabit a part but also make its impact linger beyond the end credits. Today, he’s at the top of his game, occupying a position that once was the late Irrfan’s, straddling mainstream, indie, and middle-of-the-road projects.
Nila Iyer (@nilaiyer) sits down with Bajpayee to discuss films, family, and his 30-year-long career.

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee’s (@bajpayee.manoj) filmography is a “mixed bag”, he points out—by design. His upcoming projects include ‘Governor’ directed by Chinmay Mandlekar, and a reunion with Ram Gopal Varma for the horror comedy ‘Police Station Mein Bhoot’. Neeraj Pandey’s crime thriller for Netflix is also in the pipeline, as is American director Ben Rekhi’s upcoming adaptation of Arvind Adiga’s novel ‘Last Man in Tower’.
Right now, Bajpayee is at that sweet spot in his career where he can take the long view. On screen, he has played losers and heroes, but IRL he’s able to balance celebrity with craft and Bollywood’s ruthless demands with his individual ambitions. For him, mainstream cinema is a strategic choice. “The Hindi film industry is very market-oriented, so to keep one’s value alive one has to balance it with things on which I don’t compromise,” Bajpayee explains.
Read Nila Iyer’s (@nilaiyer) full interview with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
You know the tank. The one that looks like it’s been hacked together in a downtown apartment at 2 am—a white vest, studded, laced, and scrawled with “Rock Royalty”. Stella McCartney(@stellamccartney) first made it in 1999 for herself and Liv Tyler to wear to the Met Gala (theme: Rock Style, obviously). The origin story is as scrappy as the top: a trip to a shop called Filth Mart in Little Italy, a three-pack of Fruit of The Loom vests, and a stud gun.
Now, the legendary tank top is back, this time as part of her collaboration with H&M(@hm), alongside a tightly edited run of “Stella’s superstars”. Think lace-trimmed camisoles lifted from McCartney’s Chloé days, slinky knits with an aluminium chain detail that nods to the Falabella bag, and party pieces that glitter with recycled crystals. There are easy, elevated everyday pieces, like a sharply cut grey suit and draped dresses, and then, just to keep things from getting too polite, a unicorn sweatshirt, a crystal bralette, and jeans with crystal-outlined star cutouts at the hip.
At the link in bio, @chloechou13 spills all the deets on the collection everyone’s waiting for.
Images courtesy: @hm

posts.caption:
There was a time, not too long ago, when a man’s daily carry was limited to the holy trinity: phone, wallet, keys. Anything more required a backpack that screamed “I’m heading to a 9 am lecture” or a briefcase that said “I’m a mid-level manager and I have a mortgage.”
But look around today and the Man Purse has fractured into a dozen sub-genres. It’s no longer just about utility; it’s about a specific kind of soft-launching of one’s personality. Whether it’s the Big Tote Energy of the creative class or the micro-sling of a film producer—those tiny, zipped pouches that suggest the wearer has reached a state of zen-like minimalism—these bags are the new windows into the male psyche.
But what is actually in there? At the link in bio, @patsyanthonyy cornered five men across the creative spectrum to play a high-stakes game of Show and Tell to finally understand what’s really in their bag.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Nod on April 15, 2026 tagging @akshatrajan. May be an image of wrist watch, glasses, magazine, poster and text that says 'n FASHION What's in the bags of stylish men? Speedos, tuning forks and doggy treats'.

posts.caption:
Over a period of five years, Sohrab Hura(@sohrabhura), winner of the 2025 Eye Art and Film Prize, recorded everyday life and the passage of time in Kashmir through three distinct phases of its winter: chillai kalan (harsh cold), chillai khurd (small cold), and chillai bache (baby cold).⁠ The result is ‘Snow’, a photographic documentation of the picturesque landscapes and communities of a place that is, ironically, also one of the most heavily militarised regions in the world. The photo tome marks the artist’s return to photography.
With ‘Kolkata’, Sri Bodanapu(@herbseedspice) continues her passion project of documenting the symphony of sights, sounds, and flavours of Indian cities via personal essays and photographs a year after the success of her culinary photo book ‘Mumbai’, which won a Gourmand Best in the World award.
At the link in bio, The Nod’s @tanviii_05 rounds up this year’s most exciting releases that will help you get acquainted with India’s art (and best-known artists). The best part? They double as decor.

posts.caption:
As luxury brands ban fur, red carpets go cruelty-free, and designers experiment with plant-based materials, the fashion industry is undergoing a visible shift. Pineapple fibre, mushroom leather, cactus-based alternatives and lab innovations are entering the conversation. Activism has pushed brands to rethink feathers, exotic skins and animal-derived materials once considered aspirational.
Yet contradictions remain. Some labels announce fur bans but don’t reveal that they continue using exotic skins. Others replace animal materials without clarifying what “vegan” actually means. The language — vegan, cruelty-free, plant-based, faux — is often used interchangeably, creating confusion for consumers trying to make ethical choices.
At the same time, awareness around animal welfare is growing, and traditional practices — from wool to leather — are being debated with renewed scrutiny.
This special report brings together perspectives from PETA India lead Poorva Joshipura, designers Anita Dongre and Ladakh based Jigmat Norbu, alongside industry reports and emerging material innovations, as an investigative snapshot of where vegan fashion stands today — and why it matters.
Is fashion moving toward meaningful change, or simply redefining the narrative?
🖋️ @shefatwork
@peta @petaindia @poorvajo @anitadongre @fashionforgood @victoriassecretindia @rewild @stellamccartney @newbottega @hermes @gucci @poloralphlauren @jigmat.couture @loomsofladakh @royalenfieldsocialmission

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption:
I fell down a rabbit hole today. It started with an artist whose images are everywhere and whose name almost nobody knows…I didn’t even recognise his name.
His name was Erté and he made 240 covers for Harper’s Bazaar. Stunning work, don’t get me wrong but I quickly realised, built entirely on visual cultures that weren’t his. Ancient Egypt, Iran, Japan, China. It was something called Orientalism.
Then I found his 1920 illustration titled “Les Fleuves” - Le Mississippi. A figure rendered in blackface, and I’d been planning a carousel about his work. I scrapped it INSTANTLY.
Blackface is NOT an isolated mistake, it’s endpoint of a worldview that treats entire cultures as decoration and the people within them as less than human.
I know this pattern from the inside from when I worked as a textile designer. I was handed briefs that said “boho” or “tribal” with mood boards mixing Javanese batik, Kashmiri paisley, chikankari embroidery from Lucknow, and West African wax print. All in the same brief.
These are not the same culture, or even the same continent. But the business didn’t care, it just needed something that wasn’t Western.
This is why I’ll be going back to the origins and the history of draping, and it starts in ancient Mesopotamia.
The Goddess Project: the history of draping the body, from ancient Mesopotamia to the present day.
Stay tuned.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on April 16, 2026. May be an illustration of poster and text that says 'Art Deco was built on stolen visual culture. So sois"boho." is boho." The fashion industry's s problem with cultural appropriation. veronicatucker veronica tucker'.

posts.caption:
As many of you, I have recently finished watching The Other Bennet Sister and I cannot stop thinking about it.
I immediately felt this protectiveness over Mary and what her character had to deal with in her family. In this adaptation we see a far crueler version of Mrs Bennet and how Mary’s experience differed so greatly from that of her sisters’.
Most adaptations have made Mary the joke. The plain one, the odd one, the embarrassment. This version reveals the vivid, multidimensional personality of Mary. Her brilliant mind, her quick wit and how she learns to put herself first after a lifetime of putting herself last.
I highly recommend this show. I laughed, I cried and I felt deeply for Mary and her arc in this story.
Well done to @ellabruccoleri for portraying Mary’s depth, kindness and unapologetic fashion sense.
And to @sianjenkinscostume for such beautiful costumes! I personally would happily wear EVERYTHING Mrs. Gardiner wore, what a colour palette.

posts.caption:
There’s a painting at the NGV I’ve been visiting for about fifteen years.
It’s called Queen Esther, painted in 1878 by Edwin Longsden Long. In it, a woman is being dressed before walking into a Persian court uninvited, an act that under the law at the time could have meant her death. Behind her, an attendant holds up an enormous piece of cloth, not draped on her yet, just held and offered to her.
That image has stayed with me longer than almost anything else I’ve encountered in fashion, because the fabric is being brought to her…not the other way around.
I somehow always end up there every time I visit.
The oldest garments in human history weren’t constructed around the body…they were draped onto it. The fabric was considered the art piece and cutting it would have been unthinkable, because producing it had taken months.
I’ve loved Greek mythology since I was young. It runs through my life in ways I didn’t always notice. I even named my son after a Titan, so perhaps none of this is a surprise 😅
The Goddess Project is a content series starting in May, tracing the history of draped dress from ancient Mesopotamia through Egypt, Greece, the Etruscans and Rome, to the designers who looked back to look forward. Madame Grès, Madeleine Vionnet, Mariano Fortuny. Designers who understood that the most radical thing you could do with fabric was listen to it and trust it ✂️
It launches alongside the Met Gala 2026, whose theme is Costume Art. And later this year, Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey arrives in cinemas. Ancient Greece is having a moment, and I’m SO here for it.
There will be a draped pattern, a fabric print collection, a making process filmed from beginning to end, and a lot of history told through the thing I understand best, which is cloth.
I can’t wait to share more with you 🫶🏼
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on April 11, 2026. May be a graphic of poster, book, magazine and text that says 'The Goddess Project Five thousand years of cloth. veronica veronicatucker tucker'.

posts.caption:
In the first two years of dating, Damini Chodagam and Arjun Arora took spontaneous trips around the world. After rewatching Zindagi Na Milegi Dobara (probably for the hundredth time) they both thought Spain would be the perfect setting. For the wedding, María del Fernández, the singer of Señorita in the film, was brought in from Málaga to perform. posts.caption:
Writing, especially professionally, can seem like an intimidating world to enter. How does one nurture their writerly ambitions when entire novels are being written by AI, published authors are being pulled up for plagiarism and readers are a dwindling breed? Any writer can assure you that their aspirations are purely fuelled by hope. And it is for these hopefuls that Vogue India is launching the Vogue Writers Club. Swipe for more details and head to the link in bio to apply.
Please note: All participants must be over 18 years old and hold an Indian bank account.

posts.caption:
What happens when you put Meryl Streep and Anna Wintour—the two Mirandas, as it were—in a room together? A wide-ranging conversation about fashion, family, friendship, and yes, the forthcoming sequel to “The Devil Wears Prada.”
“In terms of Miranda, and coming back to that character 20 years later, I did think honestly about Anna, and tried to imagine what it was like to carry her responsibility and to be as interested in the world and curious as she must have to be,” Streep tells Greta Gerwig in Vogue’s May 2026 cover story. And what did Wintour do when she heard the rumours that “The Devil Wears Prada 2” was happening? She called Streep, of course. “I knew she would tell me if it was going to be all right,” Wintour says. “I trusted her implicitly.”
Read the full story at the link in bio.
Photographed by @annieleibovitz, Styled by @therealgracecoddington, Hair by @thebobbymichael and @dmchairmakeup, Makeup by @melissasilvermakeup and @dmchairmakeup, Tailoring by @billbulldresses, Produced by AL Studio, Production Design by @not.thestate. Vogue, May 2026.

posts.caption:
“It was a risk,” says @StellaMcCartney, recalling the decision to launch her collaboration with @HM in 2005. A risk that paid off: Looking back at the headlines from the time, the release of McCartney’s collection sparked an unprecedented frenzy. So it feels like a full-circle moment for the designer that now, just over 20 years later, she’s dropping a new collection on May 7 with the Swedish retail powerhouse—one that feels almost like a miniature retrospective of her greatest hits over the intervening years. “I really wanted to revisit the Stella wardrobe, the icons,” says McCartney of the collection.
At the link in bio, the designer shares a first look at her new H&M collection—which has been almost entirely designed using organic, recycled, and next-gen sustainable fabrics—with Vogue.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 15, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, magazine and text that says 'VOGUE RUNWAY STELLA MCCARTNEY ON RETURNING TO COLLABORATE WITH H&M, 20 YEARS LATER'.

posts.caption: In "Mother Mary," does mother know best? The costume design team certainly did. “The film talks about the complexity of creative relationships,” says @IrisVanHerpen, who made the film’s grand-finale gown. “The dress becomes an expression of that—how one creative influences the other, and how you can lose or regain your identity through such a relationship.” Director David Lowery enlisted @Bina_Daigeler_costumedesign to oversee costumes for the film, which meant a complex journey for Daigeler to craft on-screen for the titular character. Tap the link in our bio to learn more about the pop star-worthy costumes worn by @AnneHathaway and the deeper story behind them.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue on April 15, 2026. May be an image of costume, headdress and text.

posts.caption: Playing a real-life person is nothing new for @AdrienBrody. Now, as he makes his Broadway debut in "The Fear of 13," the man who inspired his character is often sitting in the audience. Written by @LindseyFerrentino and starring Brody and @tessamaethompson, the play is based on David Sington’s documentary of the same name, which chronicles the 22 years Nick Yarris spent wrongfully imprisoned for murder, rape, and abduction before he was exonerated in 2003. Reflecting on the challenge of portraying someone with such an extraordinary story, Brody says of Yarris, “He has this unique ability to be both incredibly funny and gregarious, while also accessing deep emotion and empathy, all while maintaining a stoicism.” Learn more about the play and Brody’s return to Broadway at the link in bio. Photos: @emiliomadrid

posts.caption:
Is fashion art? Is art fashion? The answer to those perennial, pesky questions should be resolved this May when The Metropolitan Museum of Art’s Costume Institute, after years in the basement, expands upstairs into prime real estate, just adjacent to the @metmuseum’s Great Hall. “In a way fashion is beyond art,” says the Costume Institute’s curator in charge, Andrew Bolton. “It embodies our lived experience. It’s the only art form that does that.” Ahead of the 2026 #MetGala, head to the link in our bio for a sneak peek inside the @metcostumeinstitute’s upcoming exhibition, “Costume Art.”
Photographed by @ethanjamesgreen, Written by Dodie Kazanjian, Styled by @amandaharlech, Hair by @jimmypaulhair, Makeup by @kabukinyc, Tailoring by @carol_ai_studio, Manicures by @jinsoonchoi, Set Design by @frau.juliawagner, Produced by @specialproduction.agency. Vogue, May 2026.

posts.caption: Over the years, the Wessel & Vett prize has bolstered the success of Scandi brands including Cecilie Bahnsen, Saks Potts, and Anne Sofie Madsen. Nicklas Skovgaard took home the prize in 2025. The makeup of this year’s finalists is representative of where fashion is going globally. Berner Kühl and O. Files represent the continuing industry excitement in menswear. While all of these Danish brands work with sustainability, responsible design is central to Bonnetje’s practice. Caro Editions, meanwhile, leans into vintage and has a playfulness that’s stamped with the personality of its founder, the former model Caroline Bille Brahe. Learn more about the 2026 finalists at the link in bio.

This being so, our editors are attuned to the experience both on and around the catwalks. A rereading of the fall 2026 reviews has allowed us to compile a list of books, movies, television shows, musicians, and specific tracks that defined the season and which open other doors into the vast world of fashion. posts.caption: It was a full-circle moment for the tippet at the fall 2026 shows, with rounds of material wrapping and warming models’ arms and chests. While fur both real and faux was a preferred material, knit and bespangled options were seen on many runways. Shrug-like capelets served much the same purpose as stoles, swaddling the body in comfort or providing a sense of protection. posts.caption: The enduring pull of ’90s fashion lies in its simplicity. While trends come and go, the decade’s signature silhouettes have never really gone out of style. Among the great ’90s staples that have retained their cultural capital today are the slip dress, the cigarette jean, and the pencil skirt. Shop Vogue's edit of the decade’s defining pieces that still hold up at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
@caileespaeny is in between worlds. Having played characters that span across time and space, the Golden Globe-nominated actress has a fascinating plurality about her that feels both familiar and fresh.
Cast in a nostalgic mood that calls to mind the filmography of #DavidLynch and #StanleyKubrick, the actress transforms into a defiant, enigmatic persona as our 'Retrofuture' cover star, lensed by the legendary Canadian photographer @normanjeanroy. "For the shoot, we were tapping into a bit of a David Lynch world. It was so fun to lock into this set and character with the team on this shoot," Spaeny muses.
As one of two cover stars for April's 'Retrofuture' issue, Spaeny lets us into her universe—from her determined portrayal of Rain Carradine in 'Alien: Romulus' to the tender portrait she paints in 'Priscilla' and her newest work for @netflix's limited series 'Beef'. The full cover story, now in the link in bio.

posts.caption:
There is a certain joy when one lands the most exciting vintage finds. That moment of sheer bliss when you pick up that rare item you’ve been trawling the internet for. Now, it’s time to drum up that excitement, with #VogueSingapore’s first-ever Vogue Vintage Market.
Presented with Southeast Asia’s leading auction house @hotlotz, the inaugural market will feature over 500 different pieces of clothing, costume jewellery, accessories and more. Specially curated by the Vogue Singapore team, the selection will include rare archival pieces, as well as items from the personal collections of editor-in-chief @monkiepoo and other local fashion collectors, with part of the proceeds going to the #VogueFoundation.
Taking place on 16 April, Vogue Vintage is open to the public, and Club Vogue members will receive exclusive early access and discounts on items at our first vintage market—sign up now in the link in bio to reserve a spot.

posts.caption:
@sunnyleone exudes beauty, brilliance, and benevolence on our August Cover. A vision in a stunning silk saree, embodying the essence of grace and compassion.
Sunny's inner glow illuminates her outer radiance, proving that true beauty is more than skin deep. Behind her dazzling smile lies a sharp mind, showcasing that beauty and brains can go hand in hand. With a heart of gold, her compassion shines as brightly as her career. Witness the multi-faceted charm of a woman who's not just a stunner, but also a thinker and a doer. Sunny Leone- redefining what it means to be a true icon.

✨ Creator, Actor, PHENOMENON! ✨ On the June-July cover of Wedding Vows, we present the trailblazing @mostlysane, Prajakta Koli!
From building a YouTube empire to being the lead for Netflix Original Mismatched, from being part of Michelle Obama’s Day Time Emmy winning docu-series to being UNDP India's First Youth Climate Champion, her journey is nothing short of extraordinary.
With her debut romance novel set to launch this year, along with her first Amazon Prime Original Andhera, Prajakta’s journey is a testament to the power of dreams, hard work, and staying true to oneself. She continues to inspire millions with her authenticity, relatability, and unstoppable spirit.

posts.caption:
The Great Gatsby. Promising Young Woman. Maestro. There’s no shortage of career highlights for Carey Mulligan. Our April cover star has never shied away from portraying complicated women with rich inner lives, and now, she’s stepping into her messiest role yet. “I was like, ‘Oh, that’s insane and feral. And I loved it,’” Mulligan recalls of reading one particularly unhinged scene in season 2 of Beef, the Emmy-winning dark comedy-drama miniseries in which her character’s marriage devolves into resentment, blackmail, and regret. With the sophomore season hitting Netflix this month and Greta Gerwig’s highly anticipated Narnia adaptation coming later this year, the actress has her hands full with one buzzy project after another. From Mulligan’s real marriage advice to her dream projects, go deeper into our April cover story at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
"I remember when I read The Boys. I was like, 'What's this superhero shit?' And look where we are," @colby.minifie tells us. Minifie has just wrapped up a career-defining run on Amazon's satirical superhero drama, The Boys. What started out as a small recurring role as Ashley Barrett evolved into a "fan-favorite series regular" for seven years. "I think people connect to Ashley because they've all had a shitty boss," Minifie says. As she closes this chapter with The Boys, we chat with the actress about the transformative role and what might come next for her at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
@chanelofficial unveils the ‘Coco Game’ collection at Watches & Wonders 2026 in Geneva a playful yet precise fusion of haute horology and house mythology.
At its core sits a one-of-a-kind chess set, where every piece is reimagined through Chanel’s codes. The Vendôme column stands as the rook, the lion rules as king, and sculpted couture busts take the place of bishops. Gabrielle Chanel herself becomes the queen her time discreetly concealed beneath her iconic slingbacks.
The board is crafted in white gold and black ceramic, a sharp contrast of light and shadow. Each queen is adorned in diamond tweed a setting engineered exclusively for this creation and can be worn as a pendant on a white gold, diamond, and onyx chain. Unsurprisingly, the piece is already spoken for.
Beyond the centrepiece, the ‘Coco Game’ narrative extends across Chanel’s watchmaking icons. On the J12, Mademoiselle appears as a pixelated figure marking the seconds, precision-cut from carbon plate. On the Boy·Friend, she takes form as the Queen of Hearts, elegantly poised on the dial.
📸 : Chanel
posts.alt_text: Photo by WOMENCULTURE on April 15, 2026. May be an image of magazine, poster and text that says 'FASHION Chanel Turned Its Icons Into A Chess Set For "Coco Game" WOMEn CULTURE CUL JRE'.

posts.caption:
The @tiffanyandco Blue Book Collection 2026, titled Hidden Garden, is a bold return to nature reimagined through high jewelry at its most artistic. Inspired by the legendary Jean Schlumberger, the collection transforms birds, butterflies, and blossoms into surreal, gem-set sculptures.
Under the direction of Nathalie Verdeille, each piece feels alive featuring rare gemstones, vibrant color pairings, and intricate craftsmanship that pushes beyond traditional luxury. Standouts like the reimagined “Bird on a Rock” and fantastical insect motifs blur the line between jewelry and art.
This isn’t just a collection it’s Tiffany turning nature into power, fantasy, and legacy in its purest high-jewelry form.
📸: @tiffanyandco

posts.caption:
We all noticed the makeup at #Gucci—but it felt like the start of something bigger this season, a clear shift away from the “clean girl” aesthetic.
At #SaintLaurent and #Balenciaga, beauty turned darker and more undone: smoky eyes, smudged liner, and effortless hair bringing back a moodier, more decadent kind of glamour.
That contrast carried into the clothes. The Kering woman reads bold, rebellious, and unapologetically sensual, while LVMH houses like #Celine and #Dior leaned into softness—natural makeup, precise tailoring, and subtle nods to nature.
For once, the divide felt sharp—in the best way—offering two distinct visions of femininity.
So… are you the polished LVMH girl, or the unpredictable, dangerous Kering party girl?

posts.caption:
This 1965 collection by  @ysl Yves Saint Laurent was created in response to the work of Piet Mondrian.
Inspired by a book gifted by his mother—“Piet Mondrian, sa vie, son œuvre”—Saint Laurent translated painting into fashion.
Across 26 pieces, the collection dissolved the boundary between canvas and clothing, turning flat compositions into movement.
What emerged was not just design, but art in motion.
Swipe to discover.

posts.caption:
Is Barneys making a comeback?
Sources tell WWD that Jamie Salter, the chairman and chief executive officer of Authentic Brands Group, has been probing the possibility of reviving Barneys on Madison Avenue — in the same location where the luxury retailer operated its flagship until its 2019 bankruptcy, a process the opened up the door for Authentic to buy the company.
One source said, “There have been a few conversations happening with the landlord, about that space in particular.”
Saks Fifth Avenue had the exclusive rights for retailing Barneys in the U.S., but those rights have been released due to the bankruptcy of Saks Global in January.
Read more details at WWD.com — #linkinbio
Report: @evangclark

posts.caption:
@jakegyllenhaal is the first global male ambassador for @bvlgari.⁠
⁠
"The irony is that the very first piece of jewelry I ever bought for myself was a Bulgari watch," he told WWd. "I still have it, and I really cherish it.:⁠
⁠
In advance of @watchesandwonders 2026 in Geneva — where Bulgari will show its latest watch designs — Gyllenhaal posed for the WWD cover shoot during his quick few days in Milan, in the garden of the Bulgari Hotel.⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, the actor discusses how he treasures his first watch and the meaning it holds for him, along with his love for Italy.⁠

posts.caption:
After 10 years of writing, rewriting, and building a cult following off-Broadway and in the West End, @titaniquemusical has officially arrived on Broadway.
What began as a late-night conversation (and a couple of martinis) between friends in Los Angeles has evolved into a full-scale St. James Theatre production, led by creator, cowriter, and star @constantinerousouli.
A jukebox parody of Titanic powered by the music of Céline Dion, the show leans into camp with intention.
“ When we wrote it, we wanted to make each other laugh,” Rousouli told WWD. “So that to me is the number-one thing that I want to continue to give to people: come into our world, and let’s laugh for two hours.”
Read more from the interview at WWD.com — #linkinbio
Report: @ktauer
Photos: @lexieblacklock
